era_context: The Roman Empire is divided between Constantine I (West) and Licinius (East), with an uneasy coexistence that is beginning to show signs of strain. Constantine is consolidating power in the Western Empire and has recently defeated other rivals (Maxentius in 312 CE, Maximinus Daia in 313 CE). Licinius controls the Eastern Empire and is attempting to maintain his position. The empire is administratively fragmented, with competing power centers. Christianity is growing in influence, particularly in urban centers and among the military, though paganism remains dominant in many regions.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Rhine and Danube

events.title: Constantine's Edict on Sunday Rest
events.description: Constantine I issues an edict declaring Sunday (the Lord's Day) as a day of rest for the empire, exempting farmers and rural workers. This is one of the earliest imperial decrees recognizing the Christian Sabbath and represents a significant step in the Christianization of the empire. The edict does not explicitly forbid work on Sunday but encourages rest and worship. This decree is a landmark in the integration of Christian practice into imperial law, though it is not universally enforced and pagan religious observances continue.

sources.name: Codex Justinianus (Justinian's Code, compilation of imperial decrees)
Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Constantine's edict on Sunday rest is attested in later compilations of imperial law (Codex Justinianus) and in Eusebius of Caesarea's Life of Constantine. The exact date (321 CE) is given in some sources, but the original edict does not survive. The edict's wording and scope are reconstructed from later references.
cross_references: Part of Constantine's broader Christianization of the empire
Precedes 325-001 (Council of Nicaea, which further institutionalizes Christian practice)

events.title: Constantine's Legislation on Slavery and Manumission
events.description: Constantine I issues legislation modifying laws on slavery and manumission, influenced by Christian teachings on human dignity and freedom. The legislation allows for easier manumission of slaves in churches and grants certain protections to slaves. However, the extent of these reforms is debated; Constantine does not abolish slavery, which remains a fundamental institution of the Roman economy. The legislation represents a gradual shift in imperial policy toward Christian values, though the practical impact is limited.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Constantine's legislation on slavery is attested in later compilations of imperial law. The exact date and scope of the legislation are debated; some reforms may have been issued in different years or compiled together in later sources.
cross_references: Part of Constantine's broader Christianization of the empire

events.title: Constantine's Consolidation of Power in the Western Empire
events.description: Constantine I continues to consolidate his power in the Western Roman Empire, strengthening his administrative apparatus and military forces. By 321 CE, Constantine has eliminated all major rivals in the West (Maxentius in 312 CE, Maximinus Daia in 313 CE) and is the undisputed ruler of the Western Empire. His attention is increasingly focused on the Eastern Empire and his rivalry with Licinius. Constantine's consolidation of power is facilitated by his military victories, his adoption of Christianity, and his administrative reforms.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Constantine's consolidation of power in the Western Empire is well-attested in sources. By 321 CE, his position in the West is secure, and his attention is turning toward the East.
cross_references: Follows from 312-001 (Battle of the Milvian Bridge)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Constantine as a pious Christian emperor devoted to Christian values is complicated by several factors: (1) Constantine's edict on Sunday rest, while presented as a Christian reform, was also a pragmatic administrative measure that allowed the empire to regulate labor and maintain social order. The edict does not explicitly forbid work on Sunday and exempts farmers, suggesting practical rather than purely religious motivations. (2) Constantine's legislation on slavery, while influenced by Christian teachings, does not abolish slavery and maintains the institution as a fundamental part of the Roman economy. The reforms are limited and do not represent a wholesale transformation of Roman society. (3) Constantine's Christianization of the empire is gradual and incomplete; pagan religious practices continue, and Constantine himself maintains pagan symbols and practices (e.g., the Sol Invictus imagery on coins). (4) The sources for Constantine's reign are heavily biased toward portraying him as a Christian hero; alternative perspectives are largely lost.
historiographic_note: The year 321 CE is moderately well-documented through ecclesiastical sources (Eusebius of Caesarea) and later compilations of imperial law (Codex Justinianus). However, the original imperial decrees do not survive; they are known only through later references and compilations. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Eastern Roman Empire; events in the Western Empire, Britain, and beyond the imperial frontiers are poorly attested. The sources reflect the biases of the Christian church and Constantine's court, which had vested interests in portraying Constantine as a Christian hero and reformer. Modern scholarship has increasingly questioned the traditional narrative, emphasizing Constantine's political pragmatism and the gradual and incomplete nature of his Christianization of the empire.

graph_edges.note: Constantine's edict on Sunday rest in 321 CE was part of a broader Christianization of the empire that culminated in the Council of Nicaea in 325 CE
Constantine's consolidation of power in the Western Empire in 321 CE enabled him to launch a campaign against Licinius in 323 CE
Constantine's edict on Sunday rest and his legislation on slavery were both part of his broader Christianization of the empire
